topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with an aggressive monotony, reflecting off the polished steel of the workstations. Elias slammed his palm flat onto the desk, the sudden impact making the lukewarm coffee in his mug slosh over the rim. He stared at the screen, his jaw tight, tracing the lines of code with a trembling finger. It was the third iteration of the 'Chrono-Sync' algorithm, and it was *exactly* the same as the one he’d been wrestling with for three weeks. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling at his chest. He spun around, his voice sharp, cutting through the low drone of the ventilation system. "Who authorized this duplication? This is an absolute mockery!" Across the room, Sarah didn't look up immediately, but the slight tension in her shoulders betrayed her. When she finally did, her eyes met his, and a flicker of something—annoyance mixed with a deep, weary resignation—passed between them.

[story 2]

I felt my knuckles whiten as I gripped the edge of the workbench, the cheap laminate digging painfully into my skin. Every line of data, every meticulously crafted subroutine, was a mirror image of what I had just finished an hour ago. It was infuriating. I took a deep, ragged breath, trying to force the tremor out of my hands, but it wouldn't subside. "Are you serious?" I spat the words out, my voice dangerously low, laced with a brittle edge. I pushed my chair back so violently it scraped across the floor. I needed an explanation, a justification for this infuriating, infuriating symmetry. When I finally turned, I saw Mark leaning against a cabinet, rubbing the bridge of his nose. His expression was one of profound bewilderment, which only served to amplify the churning frustration inside me.

[story 3]

The silence in the archive room was thick, heavy enough to choke on. Liam paced the narrow aisle between the towering shelves of obsolete hardware, his movements jerky and agitated. He stopped abruptly, his breathing quickening, and stared at the schematic spread across the central table. It was his design. His breakthrough. And sitting beside it, perfectly aligned, was an identical blueprint, bearing the same unique identifier. A furious flush crept up his neck. He snatched the duplicate, his fingers flexing until the paper crinkled. "This isn't a coincidence," he ground out, his voice strained and tight. He didn't look at the person standing near the doorway; he focused only on the infuriating fact that someone—or something—had duplicated his entire effort.
